skill.name: rn-native-features
skill.description: Guidance for adding Expo React Native native features, including permissions, camera, notifications, haptics, location, storage, app state, and keyboard handling.
skill.summary: Implement Expo native APIs in React Native apps with permission-aware patterns.
skill.author: CJHarmath

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many Markdown code fences as shell execution and misread ordinary Expo examples as crypto, reconnaissance, or credential access. Manual review found no prompt injection, obfuscation, hidden execution, or malicious exfiltration intent; the only real network example is a documented Expo Push API call.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The external command detections point to Markdown fenced code examples. These snippets are educational TypeScript or Python examples, not executable skill hooks or shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Each location is a Markdown fence boundary or nearby example code. No instruction in SKILL.md asks the assistant to run these snippets automatically.

low_findings.title: Legitimate Expo Push API Example
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL is the Expo Push API endpoint in a backend notification example. It sends caller-provided notification fields to Expo, not hidden marketplace user data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network call is real, but the surrounding section clearly labels it as a push notification example. No covert destination or credential harvesting behavior is present.

low_findings.title: Mobile Storage Guidance Is Not Browser Credential Theft
low_findings.description: The storage section shows AsyncStorage for preferences and SecureStore for tokens. This is expected React Native guidance and does not access browser storage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The detected storage content is mobile app storage documentation. SecureStore is recommended for sensitive data instead of unsafe plain storage.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are Token Matches
low_findings.description: No weak cryptographic algorithm is documented or invoked at the flagged lines. The lines contain skill metadata and keyboard listener cleanup code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is a natural language description, and lines 446 and 449 remove keyboard listeners. No MD5, SHA-1, DES, or similar weak crypto use appears there.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Detection Is Keyboard UI Guidance
low_findings.description: The flagged line introduces a KeyboardAvoidingView example. It does not enumerate files, environment variables, devices, users, or operating system details.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context is React Native keyboard layout handling. There is no system information collection or reconnaissance behavior in the surrounding code.

low_findings.title: Critical Combination Heuristic Dismissed After Review
low_findings.description: The critical heuristic combined unrelated false positives. Manual review found no code execution capability, no credential exfiltration, and no obfuscated control flow.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is a single Markdown guide with visible examples. The supposed execution, credential, and crypto signals do not form a real attack chain.

content.value_statement: Expo apps need careful permission handling for native APIs. This skill provides practical patterns for camera, notifications, haptics, location, storage, app state, and keyboard behavior.
actual_capabilities: Shows permission request patterns before using camera, location, and notification APIs.
Provides Expo Camera and Image Picker examples for photo capture and gallery selection.
Explains push notification registration, foreground handling, responses, local tests, and backend sending.
Covers haptic feedback patterns for taps, confirmations, errors, warnings, and selection changes.
Includes React Native storage, app lifecycle, and keyboard handling examples.
limitations: Focuses on Expo React Native projects, not bare native iOS modules.
Examples are snippets and may need imports, styles, navigation setup, or project configuration.
Push notifications require real devices and valid Expo project identifiers.
Does not replace platform privacy review or app store compliance work.

use_cases.title: Add Camera Capture
use_cases.description: Build a permission-aware camera or image picker flow for profile photos, receipts, or content uploads.

use_cases.title: Ship Push Notifications
use_cases.description: Register devices, handle notification taps, and send test messages through Expo notification services.

use_cases.title: Improve Mobile UX
use_cases.description: Add haptics, lifecycle refreshes, keyboard avoidance, and location tracking with user consent.

prompt_templates.title: Add Camera Permission
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to add a safe camera permission flow to my Expo React Native screen. Include denied-permission handling.

prompt_templates.title: Build Photo Selection
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to implement image selection and photo capture in my Expo app. Return the selected local image URI.

prompt_templates.title: Implement Notifications
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to add Expo push notification registration, foreground handling, tap routing, and a local test notification.

prompt_templates.title: Review Native API Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review my Expo native feature implementation for permissions, device limits, cleanup, and sensitive storage choices.

output_examples.input: Add camera capture to an Expo profile screen.
output_examples.output: A permission-first camera flow with a grant prompt, denied-state handling, capture action, and local photo URI handling.

output_examples.input: Set up push notifications for match reminders.
output_examples.output: A registration plan, foreground display behavior, tap response handling, local test notification, and backend send outline.

output_examples.input: Improve a form screen on mobile devices.
output_examples.output: Keyboard avoidance guidance, dismiss behavior, app lifecycle refresh points, and haptic feedback suggestions for success and errors.

best_practices: Request permissions before accessing camera, location, notifications, or other native APIs.
Use SecureStore for tokens and credentials instead of general key-value storage.
Remove listeners and subscriptions during component cleanup to avoid leaks.
anti_patterns: Do not request sensitive permissions before users understand why they are needed.
Do not test push notifications only in simulators when physical devices are required.
Do not store large objects or credentials in AsyncStorage without a clear security reason.

faq.question: Does this skill work with Expo projects?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples focus on Expo React Native APIs such as Camera, Notifications, Haptics, Location, and SecureStore.

faq.question: Can it build bare native iOS modules?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on Expo-managed native features and React Native patterns.

faq.question: Does it cover permission handling?
faq.answer: Yes. It emphasizes checking and requesting permissions before using native device capabilities.

faq.question: Can it help with push notifications?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers registration, token retrieval, foreground behavior, tap handling, local tests, and backend sending.

faq.question: Does it include secure storage guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It distinguishes AsyncStorage for preferences from SecureStore for tokens and credentials.

faq.question: Are the examples complete production code?
faq.answer: No. They are implementation patterns that need project-specific imports, styling, navigation, and configuration.
